public.explanation: An access modifier that makes a class, method, or field accessible from anywhere in the program.

private.explanation: An access modifier that makes a method or field accessible only from inside the same class.

protected.explanation: An access modifier that makes a method or field accessible within the same package and by subclasses.

class.explanation: Defines a blueprint for creating objects that share fields (data) and methods (behavior).

interface.explanation: Declares a set of method signatures that implementing classes are required to provide, without any implementation itself.

abstract.explanation: Marks a class that can't be instantiated directly, or a method with no body that subclasses must implement.

final.explanation: Marks a class that can't be subclassed, a method that can't be overridden, or a variable that can't be reassigned.

static.explanation: Marks a field or method as belonging to the class itself, rather than to individual instances of it.

void.explanation: Used as a method's return type to indicate it doesn't return any value.

new.explanation: Creates a new instance (object) from a class.

this.explanation: Refers to the current object instance a method is running on, often used to distinguish a field from a parameter of the same name.

super.explanation: Refers to the parent class, used to call its constructor or an overridden method from a subclass.

extends.explanation: Makes one class inherit fields and methods from another (parent) class, or one interface extend another.

implements.explanation: Declares that a class provides implementations for all methods required by one or more interfaces.

package.explanation: Groups related classes together under a named namespace to organize code and avoid naming collisions.

import.explanation: Brings a class or an entire package from Java's standard library (or another package) into scope so it can be used without its full name.

if.explanation: Runs a block of code only when a condition is true.

else.explanation: Runs a block of code when the preceding if (or else if) condition was false.

for.explanation: A loop that repeats a block of code a set number of times, using a counter variable (init; condition; step).

for-each.explanation: A simplified for loop that iterates directly over the elements of an array or collection.

while.explanation: Repeats a block of code as long as a condition stays true, checked before each iteration.

do-while.explanation: Like a while loop, but the code runs once before the condition is checked, so it always executes at least once.

switch.explanation: Compares a value against several possible cases and runs the matching block.

case.explanation: Defines one branch to match against inside a switch statement.

break.explanation: Immediately exits the nearest loop or switch statement.

continue.explanation: Skips the rest of the current loop iteration and moves on to the next one.

return.explanation: Exits a method and optionally sends a value back to whoever called it.

try.explanation: Marks a block of code to attempt running, so that if it throws an exception, you can catch and handle it instead of crashing.

catch.explanation: Runs when the matching try block throws a matching exception type, giving you access to the exception object.

finally.explanation: Runs after the try/catch, whether or not an exception occurred — useful for cleanup like closing resources.

throw.explanation: Raises an exception on purpose, stopping normal execution unless it's caught by a matching catch block.

throws.explanation: Declares in a method's signature that it might throw a checked exception, so callers know they need to handle it.

enum.explanation: Defines a fixed set of named constant values, useful when a variable should only ever be one of a few known options.
enum.example:
enum Color { RED, GREEN, BLUE }
Color c = Color.GREEN;

instanceof.explanation: Checks whether an object is an instance of a specific class (or one of its subclasses).

null.explanation: Represents the intentional absence of an object reference — a variable pointing to 'nothing'.

true.explanation: One of Java's two boolean literal values, representing a true condition.

false.explanation: One of Java's two boolean literal values, representing a false condition.

var.explanation: Lets the compiler infer a local variable's type from its initializer, instead of writing the type explicitly (Java 10+).

synchronized.explanation: Marks a method or block so only one thread can execute it at a time, preventing race conditions on shared data.

record.explanation: A concise way to declare an immutable data-holding class, automatically generating a constructor, getters, equals, hashCode, and toString (Java 16+).
record.example:
record Point(int x, int y) {}
Point p = new Point(3, 4);

generics.explanation: The <T> syntax lets a class or method work with any type while still keeping compile-time type safety.
